topic: Misc - Reading and Writing proc-macro2 TokenStreams

turns.content: I'm trying to parse some code in my build script using the token stream stuff, but I keep getting weird errors when I try to write it back out. It seems like just dumping the tokens isn't enough. Is there a better way to handle this?

The issue is that you're treating TokenStream exactly as if it were already formatted code, which won't work since it's just raw bytes. You need to use syn to parse those tokens into an Ast first, then re-serialize the Ast back out using serde_json or similar serialization traits to get valid Rust syntax again. The key is understanding that you can't just write TokenStream directly without proper parsing and reconstruction steps. Did you know about prettyplease formatting?

Oh right, I remember something about prettyplease but I'm not sure how it applies here since I'm outside of a proc-macro context. So does prettyplease only work inside macro expansions or can it be used in build scripts too? What exactly is the difference between using syn directly versus relying on that formatting feature?

Prettyplease actually works fine in your build script context, but you need to ensure you're parsing with the right options enabled. The key insight is that prettyplease automatically formats code when serializing a Tree or Ast back into tokens, so it's much more reliable than raw TokenStream dumping. You should parse your input using syn::parse with parser settings enabled for formatting, then serialize the resulting Ast to get clean output without manual string manipulation tricks.

That makes sense about parsing first though. But wait, what if I just want to read a file and pass those tokens around as-is? Like, can I avoid converting to an Ast at all since that seems like extra work for simple token passing? What exactly does TokenStream represent when it's not being parsed into something else?

You're right that TokenStream represents raw stream data, but the problem is you can't just pass it around expecting syntax preservation without parsing first. The whole point of converting to an Ast is because TokenStream doesn't guarantee valid Rust structure on its own - it might contain invalid tokens or missing pieces depending on how you constructed it. If you want to maintain roundtripping capabilities, you absolutely need the intermediate Ast step regardless of whether your original code was already pretty-printed or not.

Okay so I can't just pass TokenStream around directly for roundtrip purposes? What exactly happens if I try to serialize a TokenStream without going through an Ast first using serde_json or similar traits? Does it just produce garbage output that won't compile? Is there any way to bypass the Ast entirely and still get formatting back?

Yes, trying to serialize raw TokenStream directly will definitely produce invalid Rust code since it lacks proper structure. There's no shortcut around the Ast conversion because the syntax tree contains all the spacing and structural information needed for valid output. The only way to avoid manual formatting logic is to rely on prettyplease which handles this internally during serialization of the Ast, so you still need that intermediate step even if you're just doing basic roundtripping.

So basically I have to parse into an Ast and then serialize it back? That seems like two extra steps compared to what I was hoping for. Can't I just use some helper function or trait implementation from the ecosystem that does this automatically without me manually calling parse and serialize separately each time? Is there a crate that wraps all of this together?

There isn't really a single crate that fully encapsulates both parsing and formatting into one call since these are fundamentally different operations requiring different type representations. However, you can chain syn's parser with prettyplease's formatter in your own code to create this workflow automatically. The pattern is straightforward: read tokens, parse them into an Ast using the appropriate settings, then serialize that Ast back to TokenStream without manual formatting logic. This gives you exactly what you need for roundtripping while keeping things simple.

Alright so I should just use syn with prettyplease enabled and let it handle everything? But wait, earlier I thought I couldn't do this in build scripts since those aren't proc-macro contexts. Can I actually use the same approach here or is there something different about how build scripts interact with these types of things compared to actual macro expansions?

You can absolutely use the same approach in your build script - the distinction between proc-macro context and regular Rust code doesn't change how token streams behave fundamentally. Build scripts are just regular Rust programs running on your system, so they have full access to all these crates including syn and prettyplease. The only difference is that you're not dealing with the macro expansion pipeline itself, but the underlying token manipulation mechanics remain identical regardless of where your code runs.

Okay so I can use this in my build script too then? One more thing though - what happens if someone passes me a malformed TokenStream as input? Like, what exactly does syn do when it encounters invalid tokens or incomplete structures during the parsing phase? Does it just crash immediately or are there error handling mechanisms built into these types of operations?

Syn provides robust error handling through its Result type which returns detailed parse errors whenever you encounter malformed tokens. It won't just crash silently - instead, you'll get specific information about what went wrong along with suggestions for fixing it. You can catch these errors and provide meaningful feedback to your users rather than letting the program fail unexpectedly. This makes testing roundtripping much more reliable since you know exactly where parsing issues occur before attempting serialization.

That's really helpful knowing that syn gives specific error details instead of just failing. So my workflow should be: read tokens, attempt parse with error handling, if successful then serialize using prettyplease? What about performance though - does this extra roundtrip through the Ast significantly slow down build scripts or is it acceptable overhead for most cases? Is there any way to optimize this process further without losing correctness guarantees?

The AST conversion does add some overhead but it's generally acceptable for most build script use cases where code quality matters more than milliseconds. If performance becomes a concern, you could potentially skip the Ast step and manually handle formatting logic yourself using syn's tokenizer utilities to construct valid syntax strings directly from your tokens without relying on prettyplease's formatter. However, this approach requires more manual effort since you're responsible for ensuring proper spacing and structure rather than letting the library handle it automatically.

Hmm, so if I skip the Ast step I'll have to manually manage formatting logic which defeats some of the simplicity benefits? What are the trade-offs between relying on prettyplease versus implementing custom formatting handlers myself when dealing with TokenStreams in production scenarios? Are there specific edge cases where one approach fails while the other succeeds?

The main advantage of using prettyplease is that it handles all the complex spacing and bracket balancing automatically, whereas manual implementation requires you to handle every edge case yourself including nested structures and comment preservation. Prettyplease will fail gracefully if your input isn't valid Rust syntax since it relies on proper parsing first, but a custom formatter might produce unexpected output with malformed tokens unless you implement extensive validation logic. For production scenarios where reliability matters most, relying on the ecosystem tools like prettyplease is definitely worth the slight performance cost.

Thanks for clarifying all that about trade-offs and edge cases. I think I'm going to stick with using syn plus prettyplease since it seems more reliable even if slightly slower. One last question though - what exactly does TokenStream represent when it's not being parsed into something else? Can you give me a concrete mental model of what the actual data structure looks like underneath all this?

TokenStream essentially represents a sequence of token objects stored in memory, where each token is either an identifier, operator, punctuation mark, or keyword. When you serialize it without proper parsing first, you get raw sequences that might include invalid syntax patterns. The mental model should be: TokenStream = ordered list of tokens; Ast = structured representation with spacing and hierarchy info; prettyplease = formatter that converts Ast back to properly formatted TokenStream sequence.

topic: Misc - Reading and Writing proc-macro2 TokenStreams

turns.content: I'm trying to read a macro definition from a file in my build script using the `syn` crate, but I can't figure out how to parse it. The tutorial says I should use `proc_macro2::TokenStream`, but I don't understand why that's needed instead of just passing a string directly to `syn::parse`. Can you explain what happens if I try to feed a raw String into the parser without converting it first?

That is a common point of confusion. When you have a macro definition as text, you need to represent that text as a sequence of tokens before parsing. The `proc_macro2::TokenStream` type acts like an immutable list of lexical items (keywords, identifiers, punctuation) which the `syn` crate expects as input for its parser. If you pass a String directly without converting it through `TokenStream`, the parser won't recognize the structure because it needs to see individual tokens rather than raw characters. Think of it like this: if you want to build a house from bricks, you can't just hand the blueprint (the string) to the builder; you first need to lay out all the individual bricks (tokens) in order so they can be assembled correctly into walls and floors.

Okay, so I convert the String to TokenStream using `TokenStream::from_str`, then pass that to `syn::parse`. But here's my real problem: after parsing with syn, I get a parse result. How do I go backwards? If I want to write this back out as source code or verify it looks like what was originally written, how do I convert the parsed AST back into a TokenStream so I can pretty-print it?

You are absolutely right to ask about round-tripping. Once you have successfully parsed your macro definition using `syn`, you will get an AST node representing that structure. To write this back out, you cannot simply use the original String anymore; instead, you must walk through the AST nodes generated by syn and reconstruct a new sequence of tokens. You would typically iterate over the children of each node in your parsed result (like struct fields or macro arguments), collect them into a buffer, and then pass that reconstructed token sequence back to `proc_macro2::TokenStream`. This allows you to create a fresh TokenStream containing all the original information from the AST without losing syntax details.

I tried reconstructing the tokens by walking the syn nodes manually, but the output doesn't match my input exactly. I get extra whitespace or formatting differences that make the round-trip fail when I try to re-parse it again. Is this because `syn` just stores data structures and forgets about formatting rules? Can I use a crate like prettyplease to fix this?

You have hit exactly on the right tool for the job. The issue you are facing is that `syn` parses tokens into an internal data structure (like a parse tree) but does not inherently preserve the original whitespace or formatting of the source text. When you manually reconstruct tokens, you often lose those details unless you specifically copy them from the original TokenStream before parsing. You can use `prettyplease::parse` which is designed to work with `syn`. It takes your parsed AST and automatically formats it back into a valid TokenStream that preserves the original whitespace and layout exactly as written in your source file, effectively solving the round-trip problem you are struggling with.

That sounds perfect. But wait, I'm using `proc_macro2` outside of a macro context in my library code (not inside a proc macro). Does `prettyplease` work just fine there? And what if I need to generate new code dynamically instead of just pretty-printing existing input? Should I be looking at something like `quote::quote` for that?

Yes, `prettyplease` works perfectly outside the context of a proc macro because it operates on the same token format that `syn` uses. It can take any valid TokenStream and return a properly formatted version. However, if you want to generate new code dynamically rather than just formatting existing input, then yes, you should look at `quote::quote`. That crate provides macros (like `quote!`) which let you build TokenStreams programmatically by combining token pieces together using the quote syntax or the builder pattern. This is how you would construct a completely new macro definition from scratch in your library code instead of parsing and reformatting an existing file.

I see, so `quote::quote` builds tokens while `prettyplease` formats them? One more thing: I noticed that my TokenStream sometimes has trailing whitespace or comments attached to the end. When I use these in tests, those extra things confuse the test runner because it expects clean input. How do I strip out comments and ensure my stream is always clean for testing purposes?

That is a valid concern when working with TokenStreams in unit tests. You can remove comments from your TokenStream by first parsing it into an AST using `syn`, then walking through the nodes to collect only the relevant tokens while skipping over comment-related structures (like `Token::Comment`). Alternatively, you could use `prettyplease` to format your stream; since prettyplease parses and re-emits the code, comments will be preserved in their original positions. For a cleaner approach specifically for tests, you might want to manually iterate through your TokenStream, filtering out any tokens that are identified as comments or trailing whitespace before passing it into your test assertion logic.

Thanks for clarifying the comment handling. But I still have one last question: what if my input contains macro invocations with complex nesting? Like a macro call inside another macro call, and then those results need to be passed as arguments to a third macro. How do I ensure that when I parse this deeply nested structure with syn and then try to round-trip it back using `prettyplease`, the nesting order is preserved exactly?

The good news is that both `syn` and `prettyplease` are designed specifically to handle complex nesting correctly. When you parse a deeply nested macro definition, `syn` will build a hierarchical AST where parent nodes contain references to child nodes representing the inner macros and their arguments. This structure preserves the exact order of operations regardless of how deep the nesting goes. When `prettyplease` takes this AST and formats it back into a TokenStream, it walks through that same hierarchy in the correct sequence, ensuring that macro calls appear in the right places with all nested arguments properly positioned relative to each other. The round-trip will maintain perfect structural integrity as long as your original input was valid syntax.
